flashcards.back: The degree to which software meets specified requirements and user expectations. It encompasses functionality, performance, reliability, and maintainability.

A model with 11 quality factors organized in 3 categories: Product Operation (correctness, reliability, efficiency, integrity, usability), Product Revision (maintainability, flexibility, testability), and Product Transition (portability, reusability, interoperability).

flashcards.front: What are the main quality attributes in ISO 9126?
flashcards.back: Six attributes: Functionality, Reliability, Usability, Efficiency, Maintainability, and Portability. Each has sub-characteristics.
The degree to which software performs its specified functions accurately and completely without producing erroneous outputs.
The probability that software will perform its intended function without failure for a specified period under stated conditions.
The degree to which software is easy to learn, operate, and understand. Includes user-friendliness and aesthetic design.

flashcards.front: What is Maintainability in software quality?
flashcards.back: The ease with which software can be modified to correct defects, improve performance, or adapt to changing requirements.
The ease with which software can be transferred from one environment (hardware/software) to another without significant changes.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between QA and QC?
flashcards.back: Quality Assurance (QA) focuses on process improvement and prevention of defects. Quality Control (QC) focuses on detecting and fixing defects in the product.
The relationship between software performance and the amount of resources (time, memory, bandwidth) used to accomplish tasks.
The degree to which access to software or data is controlled to prevent unauthorized access, modification, or destruction.
The extent to which software components can be used in different applications or projects without significant modification.
An extended version of ISO 9126 with 8 quality characteristics: Functional suitability, Performance efficiency, Compatibility, Usability, Reliability, Security, Maintainability, and Portability.
The ease with which software can be tested to detect defects, including controllability, observability, and simplicity.
The ease with which software can be adapted for different environments or extended with new features without degrading existing functionality.
